moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, immediately staking a claim to the central e5 and c5 squares while preparing to develop our pieces with maximum flexibility. This move is the starting point of many of chess's most principled openings, where we aim to build a strong pawn center and coordinate our pieces harmoniously. The d4 opening typically leads to more positional, strategic games compared to 1.e4, giving us excellent control over the center and multiple development options for our pieces.

Black immediately challenges our central pawn with the knight, attacking d4 and preparing to fight for central control from the very first move. This hypermodern approach shows that our opponent isn't interested in occupying the center with pawns right away, but rather wants to attack it from the flanks with pieces. The knight on f6 also prepares kingside castling and keeps Black's options open for various defensive setups, including the King's Indian, Nimzo-Indian, or Grünfeld Defense.

We're playing the English Opening move order, supporting our d4 pawn while simultaneously controlling the crucial b5 and d5 squares and preparing to challenge any central pawn advances by Black. This move is the hallmark of the Queen's Gambit family of openings, where we're willing to offer space and central control in exchange for rapid piece development and long-term positional pressure. The c4 pawn also opens lines for our queen and light-squared bishop while beginning to restrict Black's pawn breaks on the queenside.

Our opponent is signaling their intention to fianchetto the dark-squared bishop on g7, adopting a hypermodern setup that will put tremendous pressure on our central pawns from the long diagonal. This move prepares Bg7 and indicates we're likely heading into either a King's Indian Defense or Grünfeld Defense structure, both of which are incredibly sharp and tactical openings. Black is essentially saying they're comfortable allowing us to build a large pawn center in exchange for dynamic piece activity and counterplay against it.

This is the defining move of the Grünfeld Defense! Black immediately strikes at our pawn center, offering to trade their d-pawn for our c-pawn and completely transform the pawn structure. Our opponent is embracing the Grünfeld philosophy: they're willing to give us a strong pawn center with pawns on d4 and e4 (after we recapture) in exchange for incredibly active piece play and long-term pressure against our central pawns. This move creates immediate tension and forces us to make important decisions about how to maintain our central advantage.
